record.text: This is the thing that pisses me off most about the use of AI writing.

value.text: The writing world is already pretty toxic (YA especially), so what happens when somebody runs a book written by somebody that they don't like through one of these AI checkers, gets even a 1% result and spreads rumours that a book was AI generated that can't be disproven?

Angsty pretentious teenage me writing a dream journal near christmas 1987 shows up as 95-100% AI generated, by detectors offering to sell me services to re-generate my text with AI more humanised so it passes as not AI.

It hurts to think about — even more than reading 16 year old me.

images.alt:
A screenshot of a text editor window with the title 'dream christmas year 10' (which would have been December 198 or 1986 depending how I saw myself) containing the following text:

The bathroom was all tiles, white but not really white, like they had forgotten what
clean was a long time ago. The walls were too straight and the floor was too perfect, like it had been made by something that didn’t understand people only shapes. There
were no windows just a door that didn’t have a handle on my side, which felt like a
mistake that was on purpose. the light above me buzzed like it was thinking, and I remember noticing how the sound echoes in a way that made me feel like I was smaller
than I should be. Then I saw the water. At first it was just quiet, slipping up through the drains like it was after me like it was checking the room the same way I was. But it didn’t stop. It kept rising, slow but certain, like it knew something I didn’t. I thought about turning the taps off even
though they weren’t on, which made me feel stupid but also like maybe I was missing something important. The water reflected everything perfectly, like it was trying to become the room instead of just being in it, and I had this thought that maybe I wasn’t trapped in the bathroom, maybe I was trapped in the
reflection of it. When it reached my ankles it was colder than it should have been, like it had come from somewhere deeper than pipes. I tried the door again, even though I knew it wouldn’t open, because sometimes knowing something doesn’t stop you from hoping its
wrong. The water kept rising and the light kept buzzing and the walls stayed exactly
the same, and I realised nothing in the room was going to change except me. I wondered if that was the point, like maybe the room wasn’t trying to drown me, it was trying to show me something about being stuck, or about how things fill up slowly until there’s
no space left to think. And then I woke up before I could find out if I could still
breathe.
A screenshot of the results of an LLM detecting app online that tells me my dream from 1987 is AI generated, and offers to sell me a solution to Humanize it using another AI generator
A screenshot from another AI detector saying my dream from 1987 is 100% AI generated and offers to sell me more advanced sentence scanning.
Another screenshot of another AI detector app that says my dream from 1987 is 95% AI, then and shows a 'humanised' version generated with an AI that it claims to now be 3.2% ai.

images.alt: The visual is a cinematic, moody shot taken at night on a slick, rain-soaked city street corner reflecting the blurry red and yellow of traffic lights. I am depicted as a life-sized marionette puppet, with visible wooden joints at my elbows and knees and pale, painted porcelain skin. I have messy black and white yarn hair that’s getting wet in the rain, and heavy black theatrical makeup that is streaking down my cheeks like tears. I’m wearing a tattered, oversized pastel pink coat that’s too big for my frame, the cuffs dragging in the puddle. Above me, thick, visible strings stretch up into the dark, rainy sky, attaching to my wrists and head, looking impossibly tight. My expression is blank and empty, black glass eyes staring into nothing. The vibe is haunting, fragile, and deeply lonely—a doll realizing it was made for nothing but to be played with.

images.alt: First-person POV selfie, deep in the Pandoran rainforest at night. I am looking down at my own blue, Na'vi-like hands, but the skin is bubbling and necrotic, exposing raw, bioluminescent muscle beneath. The neural queue that usually connects to the wildlife is severed and dangling limp, dripping black fluid. Around me, the usually glowing flora is a harsh, sickening purple and acidic green, closing in tight like a cage. In the reflection of a puddle below, my face is gaunt, the yellow eyes wide and terrified. The atmosphere is suffocating, the alien beauty warped into a nightmare of biological terror and parasitic infection.

Around me, the usually glowing flora is a harsh, sickening purple and acidic green, closing in tight like a cage. In the reflection of a puddle below, my face is gaunt, the yellow eyes wide and terrified. The atmosphere is suffocating, the alien beauty warped into a nightmare of biological terror and parasitic infection.
